briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Change How Advisory Body Rules Are Set and Updated
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would change who controls the rules — called 'Terms of Reference' — for the City's advisory bodies (committees that advise Council). Under the proposed change, the Mayor would direct staff to prepare these rules, and both the Mayor, Council, or the City's top administrator could request updates — but all changes would first go through the Executive Committee before Council gives final approval.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the final adoption vote — this affects how Burnaby advisory bodies that may impact your business are governed.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Procedure Bylaw 2024 Amendment No.1 — final adoption vote

briefs.industries: Professional Services, Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to temporarily cut plumbing permit fees for some developments
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw that would temporarily reduce plumbing permit fees for most developments (excluding small-scale multi-unit housing and tenant improvements). Under the proposal, fees for a first fixture would drop from $121 to $70, and each additional fixture from $67 to $39, for permits issued on or before June 30, 2026. Owners who already paid the higher fee between January 1 and March 10, 2026 could receive a refund for the difference.
briefs.action_required: Apply for your plumbing permit before June 30, 2026 to lock in the reduced fee if this bylaw passes.

key_dates.label: Burnaby reduced plumbing permit fee window closes — deadline to apply

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Set $500 Fines for Bear Spray Sales Violations
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would add $500 fines for businesses that break rules around selling bear spray — including selling to anyone under 19, failing to check photo ID, or not keeping proper sales records. These fines would have no discount option and no compliance agreement available, meaning the full $500 applies if a violation is found.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray, review your ID-checking and record-keeping practices before this bylaw is adopted.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Bylaw Would Restrict Who Can Sell Bear Spray
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw amendment that would limit bear spray sales in Burnaby to outdoor and sporting goods retailers only. If approved, these sellers would need to check photo ID, refuse sales to anyone under 19, keep bear spray locked up, and maintain detailed sales records for at least 12 months.
briefs.action_required: If you sell bear spray, check whether your business qualifies as an 'authorized vendor' under the proposed rules.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Bear Spray Bylaw — final adoption vote (council reconsidering)

briefs.title: Council to vote on final approval of mid-rise rental building at 5889 Barker Avenue, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on the final adoption of a rezoning bylaw (Bylaw 14782, Amendment No. 19) that would allow a mid-rise rental apartment building at 5889 Barker Avenue in the Metrotown area. The project, proposed by Concord Greenhouse (Nominee) Ltd., would include 106 rental units — 48 of which would be non-market (below-market-rate) rentals.
briefs.action_required: If this development affects your business or property nearby, attend the March 10, 2026 council meeting to have your say.

key_dates.label: Burnaby REZ 25-15 rezoning bylaw final adoption — council vote

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Zoning Amendment for Barker + Olive Development Site Up for Approval
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw (No. 14782) that would amend Burnaby's zoning map to update the rules for a specific piece of land tied to the 'Amended Barker + Olive' development plan. The change would bring the site's zoning — meaning the official rules about what can be built or used there — in line with a revised development plan prepared by IBI Group.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council adoption vote in early 2026 — this could affect nearby property or business plans.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Amendment Bylaw No. 14782 — third reading passed
Burnaby Zoning Amendment Bylaw No. 14782 — final adoption vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on final approval of six-storey self-storage facility at Still Creek Avenue
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on the final adoption of a rezoning bylaw (Bylaw 14763) that would allow a six-storey self-storage building with underground parking to be built at 3942–3944 Still Creek Avenue. The applicant has met all required conditions set by the City, and the Planning Department is recommending Council give the bylaw its final approval at the March 10, 2026 meeting.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council decision on March 10, 2026 — this rezoning could affect nearby businesses.

key_dates.label: Burnaby REZ 25-05 rezoning bylaw final adoption vote — Council meeting

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on rezoning for self-storage facility at Still Creek
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would rezone two properties at 3942 and 3944 Still Creek Avenue in Burnaby to allow an amended self-storage development. The rezoning — a change to the rules about how land can be used — would update an existing development plan to reflect new building designs prepared by Studio One Architecture Inc.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the final council adoption vote in 2026 — this could affect nearby property and business owners.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Amendment Bylaw No. 14763 — final adoption vote scheduled

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Transportation & Logistics

briefs.title: Council to vote on final approval of Edmonds rental tower and community hub rezoning
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on the final adoption of a rezoning bylaw (Bylaw 14744) that would allow a residential rental tower with a community hub to be built at 7135 Walker Ave. and 7244 Arcola St. in the Edmonds neighbourhood. The applicant, Gracorp Properties LP, has met all the conditions set by the City — including engineering studies, legal agreements, and heritage documentation for the Edmonds Baptist Church — and staff are now recommending Council give the bylaw its final approval on March 10, 2026.
briefs.action_required: Watch the March 10, 2026 Council meeting — this rezoning vote could affect nearby property and business owners.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Bylaw 14744 rezoning final adoption vote — Council meeting

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Rezoning of Two Properties on Walker and Arcola Streets
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw (No. 14744) that would rezone two properties — 7135 Walker Street and 7244 Arcola Street — in Burnaby. If approved, the rezoning would change what can be built or used on those lands, and any future development would have to follow new guidelines attached to the bylaw.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the final council adoption vote — this rezoning could affect nearby property use and development plans.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw No. 14744 — third reading passed, final adoption vote pending

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment to Create New Employment, Industrial, and Agricultural Districts
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on Bylaw No. 14792, which would update Burnaby's zoning rules by creating several new land-use zones — including an Employment District (E1), Industrial District (I1), Interim Parks/Open Spaces District (PM1), Interim Institutional District (PM2), and Interim Agricultural District (AM1). The proposed bylaw would also add or update dozens of definitions (such as 'light industrial,' 'commissary kitchen,' and 'works yard') and adjust rules around building height, fencing, parking, home-based businesses, and short-term rentals across many existing zones.
briefs.action_required: Check if your property or business is in an affected zone — this could change what uses are permitted on your lot.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw Amendment No. 2, 2026 — third reading scheduled

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on energy-based fees for public EV chargers
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on switching Burnaby's public EV charging stations from time-based fees (paying by the hour) to energy-based fees (paying by the kilowatt-hour of electricity actually used). If approved, Level 2 chargers would cost $0.2865/kWh and Level 3 chargers $0.3479/kWh, plus idle fees for vehicles left plugged in after a full charge.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — it will change how much you pay to charge an EV at City-owned stations.

key_dates.label: Burnaby energy-based EV charging fees — 30-day public notice period begins after approval

briefs.title: Proposed: New Fine for Exceeding Time Limit at EV Charging Parking Spaces in Burnaby
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would add a new fine for drivers who stay too long in an electric vehicle (EV) metered parking space. Under the proposal, overstaying the maximum time limit at an EV charging meter would result in a $75 fine (reduced to $60 if paid early).
briefs.action_required: If your business has EV charging spaces, watch for this decision — it affects how customers use those spots.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update EV charging fees at city-owned parking stations
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would replace the current EV charging fee table with two new rate structures: one based on energy used (per kWh) and one based on time parked. Under the proposal, Level 2 chargers would cost $0.2865/kWh and Level 3 chargers $0.3479/kWh, with idle fees — extra charges for leaving your vehicle plugged in after charging — kicking in after a 5-minute grace period.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council vote — this could affect your costs if your business or staff use Burnaby's public EV chargers.

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update EV charging meter rules and fee structures
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a bylaw amendment that would update how electric vehicle (EV) charging meters work in Burnaby. The proposed changes would allow fees to be charged either by time or by energy used (kilowatt-hours), update the technical definition of EV charging meters to include faster Level 3 chargers (up to 350 kW), and add a new rule that makes it illegal to park in an EV charging space beyond a maximum time limit set by the City's Director of Engineering.
briefs.action_required: If you operate EV charging stations or a parking lot in Burnaby, watch for the council vote and review the new fee rules.
briefs.industries: Transportation & Logistics, Retail, Landlord & Property Mgmt

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby RCMP Status Report on Crime and Policing, August–November 2025
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive and review a status report from the RCMP covering policing activity from August to November 2025, plus a quarterly performance update for July to September 2025. The report is for information only — no new rules or bylaws are being voted on — but it highlights crime trends affecting local businesses, including a rise in robberies, thefts from vehicles, and break-and-enters at non-residential properties.
briefs.action_required: Review the robbery and theft trends in this report — check if your business area is affected and update your security plan.

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Burnaby Fire Department Status Update for December 2025
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a status update report from the Burnaby Fire Department covering activities through the end of December 2025. The report outlines incident statistics, fire prevention inspections, training hours, and community outreach — it is being presented for information only and does not propose any new rules or requirements for businesses.
briefs.action_required: No action needed — monitor future council meetings if fire inspection or code enforcement changes are proposed.

briefs.title: Council to consider status update on Burnaby–New Westminster Youth Exploitation Task Force
briefs.summary: Council is set to receive a status update on the Burnaby–New Westminster Task Force on Sexually Exploited and At-Risk Youth. The report covers the Task Force's recent activities — including a November 2025 professional development day on online sexual exploitation — and outlines plans to continue meetings and workshops in 2026.
briefs.action_required: If you work with youth, watch for future Task Force workshops on online safety and exploitation prevention.

briefs.title: Council to Consider Updates on Major Burnaby Recreation and Civic Building Projects
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive status updates on several large civic construction projects, including a new recreation centre at Burnaby Lake, the Cameron Community Centre and Library, a new RCMP building, and more. Council will also consider a staffing and operations plan — called 'People-Play' — to prepare for opening three new recreation centres that will nearly double the city's recreation services by 2030.
briefs.action_required: Watch for Council's direction on recreation centre opening scenarios and budgets — decisions could affect local hiring and contracts.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre — projected handover to Parks, Recreation and Culture
Cameron Community Centre and Library — projected handover to Parks, Recreation and Culture
Brentwood Community Centre — projected construction start
Brentwood Community Centre — projected handover to Parks, Recreation and Culture
People-Play phased opening scenarios presented to Council for budget direction
James Cowan Theatre — projected handover to Parks, Recreation and Culture
Community Safety Building (RCMP) — projected construction completion
Mintara Restaurant Expansion — projected handover to Parks, Recreation and Culture

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Labour & Employment, Food Service

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Status Update on Burnaby's 2025 Casino Gaming Reserve Spending
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive a status update on the Gaming Reserve — a special fund fed by 10% of net casino proceeds from Grand Villa Casino. The report shows the reserve received about $10.1M in casino grants in 2025, spent roughly $12.7M on city programs and projects, and ended the year with a balance of about $11.7M.
briefs.action_required: No direct action needed — monitor how gaming funds are allocated in the upcoming 2027–2031 budget process.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Gaming Reserve annual report — Province of BC submission deadline
Burnaby Council meeting to receive Gaming Reserve 2025 status update

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Status Update on Burnaby Mountain Safety Reserve Spending
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to receive a status update on the Burnaby Mountain Safety Reserve — a fund built from a $20.1 million agreement with Trans Mountain Corporation paid out over 21 years. So far, $6.7 million has been spent on three projects: an alternate access route on Burnaby Mountain, a hazardous materials response vehicle, and operating costs for Fire Station 8.
briefs.action_required: Monitor council updates if your business operates near Burnaby Mountain — new safety infrastructure is coming.
briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Transportation & Logistics, Natural Resources

briefs.title: Council to vote on supporting Tourism Burnaby's bid to host 2028 Canadian Urban Forest Conference
briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on whether to back Tourism Burnaby's plan to submit a bid to host the 2028 Canadian Urban Forest Conference — a national event drawing about 350 forestry and sustainability professionals. The support being requested is 'in principle' (meaning a green light to move forward, not a cash commitment), and no city funding is being asked for at this stage.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — it could bring a major conference and visitors to Burnaby in 2028.

key_dates.label: Tourism Burnaby bid proposal submission deadline — 2028 CUFC

briefs.industries: Hotel & Accommodation, Transportation & Logistics, Professional Services

briefs.title: Council to vote on: Burnaby's new 25-year Parks, Recreation and Culture Plan
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on approving the draft 'Making Life Better' Parks, Recreation and Culture Plan — a 25-year roadmap covering parks, recreation facilities, and cultural services across the city. If approved, Council would also authorize a second round of public engagement this spring, where residents can review and give feedback on the draft plan before a final version is brought back for approval in late spring 2026.
briefs.action_required: Attend a public engagement session this spring to give feedback on the draft plan before it is finalized.

key_dates.label: Burnaby PRC Plan — Council vote on draft approval and public engagement authorization
Burnaby PRC Plan — second round public engagement opens
Burnaby PRC Plan — public engagement findings reported to PRCC

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on Community Grant Policy and application updates
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on changes to its Community Grant Policy and application form. The proposed updates would clarify what grants can and cannot be used for, require non-profit applicants to have a Burnaby address, remove one-time events from Food Security grant eligibility, and add a new question asking how many Burnaby residents are served each month.
briefs.action_required: If you run or support a non-profit in Burnaby, review the proposed policy changes before the March 10, 2026 Council meeting.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Community Grant Policy amendments — Council vote

briefs.title: Council to vote on $580,985 ServiceNow IT ticketing software licence renewal
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on renewing software licences for the City's internal IT ticketing system — the tool staff use to submit and track IT requests. The proposed contract would go directly to ServiceNow, Inc. for roughly $581,000 (taxes included), covering three years from March 16, 2026 to March 15, 2029.
briefs.action_required: No action required — this is an internal City IT contract with no direct impact on local businesses.

key_dates.label: Burnaby ServiceNow IT licence contract start date — takes effect
Burnaby ServiceNow IT licence contract end date

briefs.title: Council to Consider Applying for Up to $200,000 in Provincial Development Approvals Grant
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on whether to apply for up to $200,000 in provincial grant funding through the UBCM Local Government Development Approvals Program (LGDAP). If approved, the money would help the City speed up building permits and development approvals — especially for small-scale housing, modular construction, and electrical service connections — making it faster and easier to get projects approved.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — faster permit approvals could affect your development or construction timelines.

briefs.title: Council to vote on granting BC Hydro access to City land at 4111 Norland Avenue
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on allowing BC Hydro to access a small portion of City-owned land at 4111 Norland Avenue. This would be done through a Statutory Right-of-Way (SRW) — a legal agreement that gives a utility company the right to install and maintain equipment on someone else's property. The access is needed to bring electrical power to the new Community Safety Building being built on the site.
briefs.action_required: No action required — this proposal affects City-owned land and does not impact private businesses.

briefs.title: Council to vote on sending concerns about proposed Metro 2050 boundary expansion options
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on forwarding a formal letter to the Metro Vancouver Board raising concerns about four proposed changes to Metro 2050 — the regional growth plan that guides land use across Metro Vancouver. The proposed changes, requested by the mayors of Surrey, Langley Township, and Delta, would make it easier to expand the Urban Containment Boundary (the line that separates urban development from rural and agricultural land), and would lower the level of regional agreement needed to approve those expansions. Burnaby's letter warns these changes could raise Development Cost Charges (fees builders and homebuyers pay for infrastructure), strain transit funding, and harm environmental targets.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Metro Vancouver Board decision in April 2026 — it could raise development costs and affect land use region-wide.

key_dates.label: Metro 2050 amendment options comment period closes — Metro Vancouver
Metro Vancouver Board initial bylaw readings — Metro 2050 amendment options
Metro Vancouver Board final reading — Metro 2050 amendment bylaw decision

briefs.title: Council to vote on liquor licence for Solo Karaoke on Kingsway, Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on whether to recommend a liquor primary licence — meaning a licence where alcohol is the main offering, not just a side item — for Solo Karaoke at 102–6462 Kingsway. The business wants to serve liquor daily from 9 a.m. to 2 a.m. for up to 80 people, and also allow minors in with a responsible adult until 10 p.m. After earlier public concerns triggered a full investigation, city staff, RCMP, and provincial regulators found no evidence of illegal liquor service and are now recommending Council support the application.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the March 10, 2026 Council decision — it could affect nearby businesses in the liquor and entertainment space.

key_dates.label: Burnaby Council vote — Solo Karaoke liquor primary licence recommendation

briefs.title: Council to Consider: Solo Karaoke Ltd. Delegation Request in Burnaby
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to hear a delegation request from Solo Karaoke Ltd., meaning the business has asked for time to speak directly to Council at an upcoming meeting. The details of what Solo Karaoke Ltd. is seeking — such as a permit, licence, or zoning change — are not fully outlined in the agenda item, but Council will consider the request on March 10, 2026.
briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council decision on March 10, 2026 — it may affect similar entertainment businesses in Burnaby.

key_dates.label: Solo Karaoke Ltd. delegation — Burnaby Council meeting date

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council Votes on Bear Spray Sales Rules and Plumbing Permit Fee Changes
briefs.summary: At its February 24, 2026 meeting, Burnaby City Council voted on several items affecting local businesses, including proposed bylaw amendments that would regulate who can sell bear spray in Burnaby, and changes to plumbing permit fees. Council also moved forward with renewing the North Road Business Improvement Area for 2026–2031 and approved new energy-based fees for city-owned EV charging stations.
briefs.action_required: Check if your business sells bear spray — new licensing rules may apply to you soon.

key_dates.label: Burnaby North Road BIA renewal period begins

briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to Replace Entire Zoning Bylaw, Effective July 1, 2026
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider a full replacement of its Zoning Bylaw — the rulebook that controls what can be built, where, and how. The proposed new bylaw would introduce clearer rules on building size, parking, landscaping, and minimum unit sizes, and is expected to come into effect on July 1, 2026.
briefs.action_required: Review the proposed Zoning Bylaw changes before April/May 2026 — new rules could affect your property or business.

key_dates.label: Burnaby new Zoning Bylaw introduced to Council — public review opportunity
Burnaby new Zoning Bylaw adoption by Council expected
Burnaby new Zoning Bylaw takes effect

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant
